meta.description: Complete cultural data for Nigeria — powering all Frica country page sections.

overview.name: Nigeria
overview.population_note: Most populous country in Africa; approximately 1 in 6 Africans is Nigerian

religion.note: Nearly equally divided. Indigenous beliefs often practiced alongside either faith (syncretism).

overview.short_description: Africa's most populous nation and largest economy, Nigeria is the beating heart of Afrobeats music, Nollywood cinema, and the ancient civilizations of the Yoruba, Igbo, and Hausa-Fulani peoples — a country often called the 'Giant of Africa'.
known_for: Afrobeats music — Burna Boy, Wizkid, Davido, Asake, Rema
Nollywood — world's 2nd largest film industry by volume
Jollof Rice (the West African Jollof War 😄)

history.summary: Nigeria's history stretches back thousands of years, home to some of the most sophisticated civilizations on the African continent — from the ancient Nok culture to the empires of Kanem-Bornu, the Oyo Empire, the Benin Kingdom, and the Sokoto Caliphate. The region was a major hub of trans-Saharan trade and later became devastated by the Atlantic slave trade. British colonization began in the 19th century, culminating in the 1914 amalgamation of the Northern and Southern Protectorates. Nigeria gained independence on October 1, 1960, and became a republic in 1963. A brutal civil war (Biafran War, 1967–1970) tested the nation's unity. Military rule dominated much of post-independence history until the return to civilian democratic rule in 1999.

timeline.description: One of the earliest known cultures in sub-Saharan Africa. The Nok people created sophisticated terracotta sculptures of humans and animals, the oldest sub-Saharan terracotta figures ever found. Discovered in Kaduna State.
One of the longest-lasting states in African history, centered around Lake Chad. A powerful Islamic empire and trans-Saharan trade hub that existed for nearly 1,000 years.

The Hausa established powerful, independent city-states including Kano, Katsina, Daura, Zaria (Zazzau), Gobir, Biram, and Rano — sophisticated urban centers of trade, scholarship, and Islamic learning.

One of the most powerful states in West African history. The Yoruba Oyo Empire controlled much of present-day southwest Nigeria, Benin Republic, and Togo. Known for its cavalry, complex bureaucracy, and the Alaafin kingship.

One of Africa's greatest kingdoms, centered in modern-day Edo State. The Benin Kingdom was renowned for its extraordinary bronze, brass, and ivory artworks — the famous Benin Bronzes — and a sophisticated royal court under the Oba (king).

Portuguese traders arrived in the Benin Kingdom in 1486, marking the beginning of European contact. The slave trade from the Bight of Benin and Bight of Biafra became one of the largest sources of enslaved Africans sent to the Americas.

Usman dan Fodio led a jihad against the Hausa city-states, establishing the Sokoto Caliphate — the largest empire in Africa at the time, covering much of northern Nigeria. It transformed the religious and political landscape of the North.

Britain annexed Lagos in 1861, and gradually colonized the rest of Nigeria through the Royal Niger Company and military conquest. In 1914, Lord Frederick Lugard amalgamated the Northern and Southern Protectorates into one entity — 'Nigeria' — a name coined by journalist Flora Shaw.
Nigeria gained independence from Britain on October 1, 1960. Nnamdi Azikiwe became the first President (1963), while Sir Abubakar Tafawa Balewa served as the first Prime Minister.

The Igbo-dominated southeastern region declared independence as the Republic of Biafra. The war resulted in over 1 million deaths, largely from starvation. Federal forces defeated Biafra in 1970. The war remains one of the defining traumas of Nigerian history.

Oil discovered in the Niger Delta transformed Nigeria into a petro-state. A succession of military governments ruled Nigeria, punctuated by coups and counter-coups. The period saw both rapid economic growth and severe political instability.

Nigeria returned to civilian democratic rule in 1999 under President Olusegun Obasanjo. The 4th Republic has seen successive peaceful transfers of power between political parties, marking a significant democratic milestone for the continent.

ethnic_groups.note: Nigeria has approximately 250–371 ethnic groups speaking over 500 languages. The three largest groups (Hausa-Fulani, Yoruba, and Igbo) account for approximately 60–70% of the population.

major.name: Hausa-Fulani
major.social_structure: Hierarchical — organized under Emirs and the Sokoto Caliphate system. Traditionally led by kings (Emirs). Strongly patriarchal and patrilineal.
major.origin: The Hausa are indigenous to northern Nigeria, forming city-states over 1,000 years ago. The Fulani, originally nomadic pastoralists, migrated from the west (Senegambia/Futa Jallon) and by the 19th century merged with the Hausa through intermarriage and the Usman dan Fodio jihad.
notable_facts: Hausa city-states (Kano, Katsina, Zaria) were major trans-Saharan trade centers
Converted to Islam from the 10th–11th century through Arab traders
Sokoto Caliphate (1804) was the largest empire in Africa at the time
Hausa is one of the most widely spoken languages in Africa (50+ million speakers)
Known for leather craft, textile dyeing (indigo), and trade
marriage_traditions: Kamu ceremony — the formal presentation and veiling of the bride
Fatihah (Nikkah) — Islamic marriage contract, traditionally conducted at the mosque after Friday prayers by men only
Sadaki — bride price paid in cash or kind to the bride's family
Walima — the post-wedding feast hosted by the groom's family
Henna (Lalle) — intricate henna decoration applied to the bride before the wedding
Daurin aure — the tying of the marriage knot ceremony
Gender-separated celebrations — men and women celebrate in separate spaces per Islamic tradition
beliefs: Islam (Sunni, predominantly Maliki school)
Bori spirit possession cult — a pre-Islamic spirit tradition that survived alongside Islam
Veneration of Sufi orders, particularly the Qadiriyya and Tijaniyya brotherhoods
Sheikh Usman dan Fodio revered as a great reformer and spiritual leader
Belief in jinn (spirits) and their influence on daily life

major.name: Yoruba
major.language: Yoruba (with many dialects: Oyo, Ijebu, Ekiti, Ondo, Egba, etc.)
major.religion: Mixed — Christians, Muslims, and traditional Yoruba religion (Ifa/Orisha worship) often in same families
major.social_structure: City-state based, each ruled by an Oba (hereditary king). Strong emphasis on family lineage, respect for elders, and age-grade associations. Historically the most urbanized Africans in precolonial times.
major.origin: The Yoruba trace their origin to Ile-Ife, believed to be where the god Oduduwa descended from heaven and created the earth. The Ooni of Ife is considered the spiritual head of all Yoruba. They migrated to their present lands west of the Niger River over a millennium ago.
notable_facts: Ile-Ife is considered the spiritual birthplace of the Yoruba and their gods
The Oyo Empire was one of the most powerful states in West Africa (13th–19th century)
Yoruba religion (Candomblé, Santería, Trinidad Orisha) spread to the Americas through the slave trade
One of the most urbanized precolonial African cultures
Yoruba diaspora is one of the largest African diaspora groups in Brazil, Cuba, and the USA
food: Ofada Rice with Ayamase (green pepper stew)

men: Agbada (grand flowing robe — the most prestigious attire)
women: Gele (elaborate headwrap — a Yoruba art form)
Adire (indigo tie-dye fabric, unique to Yoruba)

marriage_traditions: Introduction (Milo) — groom's family formally visits bride's family to ask for her hand
Bride Price (Eru Iyawo) — gifts including palm wine, kola nuts, schnapps, and money presented to bride's family
Prostration (Idobale) — groom and his men prostrate fully before bride's family as a mark of deep respect
Palm Wine ceremony — bride searches for groom in the crowd and offers him palm wine to confirm her choice
Aso-Ebi — matching fabric worn by guests of each family to signal their group affiliation
Gele tying — an elaborate headwrap is ceremonially tied on the bride
Traditional titles may be conferred on the groom by the bride's family
beliefs: Ifa Divination System — the Yoruba oracle system recognized by UNESCO as Intangible Cultural Heritage
Orisha worship — 401 deities including: Shango (thunder), Ogun (iron/war), Yemoja (water), Osun (river/fertility), Obatala (creation), Esu/Elegba (trickster/crossroads)
Belief in Ori — personal spiritual intuition and destiny
Egungun — ancestral masquerade tradition, believing ancestors return to interact with the living
Belief in reincarnation (Abiku — spirit children who repeatedly die young)
Oduduwa — the progenitor deity and ancestor of all Yoruba people

major.name: Igbo
major.language: Igbo (with many dialects: Onitsha, Owerri, Nnewi, Abia, Nsukka, etc.)
major.religion: Predominantly Christian (Catholic and Protestant), with traditional beliefs remaining important
major.social_structure: Traditionally republican and decentralized — no kings or centralized rulers. Power distributed among village councils of elders (Umunna — extended patrilineal clan). Title-taking (Ozo, Nze) is a major path to social prestige.
major.origin: The Igbo are believed to have originated in the Nri Kingdom, one of Nigeria's oldest kingdoms (around 900 CE). Nri is considered the spiritual homeland of the Igbo — its priests (Eze Nri) held religious authority but no military power, a unique model in African history.
notable_facts: The Nri Kingdom (c. 900 CE) is considered the oldest kingdom in Nigeria
Traditionally republican — one of Africa's most decentralized precolonial societies
The Igbo-Ukwu bronze artifacts (9th century) prove advanced metallurgy predating the Benin Bronzes
Declared independence as the Republic of Biafra in 1967 (civil war 1967–1970)
Entrepreneurial spirit — Igbo are among the most commercially active groups in Nigeria and the diaspora

men: Okpu Agu (red cap — worn by titled men)
Isi-agu shirt with wrapper and walking stick
women: Coral bead sets (Mgba — prestigious, can cost millions of naira)
Waist beads (Jigida) — symbolizing femininity and fertility
Akwete cloth (handwoven, from Akwete, Abia State)

marriage_traditions: List presentation — groom presents a list of items (palm wine, yam, goat, drinks, money) to bride's family as bride price

Igba Nkwu / Igba Nkwu Nwanyi (wine-carrying ceremony) — the heart of the Igbo traditional wedding. The bride searches for her groom in the crowd carrying a cup of palm wine; she kneels before him and offers him the cup to confirm her acceptance
Iku Aka — formal knocking on the door by the groom's family to formally announce their intention
Oji (kola nut) — breaking and sharing kola is sacred in every Igbo gathering and ceremony
New Yam must not be eaten before the first son eats it ceremonially — yam is sacred
Bride price (Ime Ego) negotiations between families — a sign of respect, not purchase
Umunna (extended family) involvement — all family members participate, making it a community affair
beliefs: Chukwu/Chineke — the Supreme Being and Creator God
Chi — personal guardian spirit and destiny assigned at birth
Ala/Ana — the earth goddess, most important deity, keeper of morality and law
Agwu — deity of divination and medicine
Ancestors (Ndi Ichie) — deeply revered; believed to watch over the living
Odinani — the traditional Igbo religious and philosophical system
Masquerades (Mmanwu) — the masked ancestral spirits that return from the spirit world during festivals
Eze Nri — the sacred priest-king who could ritually cleanse murderers and individuals with spiritual offenses

major.name: Ijaw (Izon)
major.language: Ijaw / Izon (and dialects: Nembe, Kalabari, Okrika, Ibani, etc.)
major.religion: Christianity (predominantly), with traditional water spirit beliefs
major.origin: One of the oldest indigenous groups in Nigeria; the Ijaw have lived in the Niger Delta for over 7,000 years according to some archaeological evidence. They are among the earliest inhabitants of Nigeria.
notable_facts: One of the oldest peoples in Nigeria, inhabiting the Delta for millennia
Powerful trading middlemen during the 18th–19th century Atlantic slave and palm oil trade
Kalabari and Nembe city-states were major trading powers
The Niger Delta oil conflict involves Ijaw communities seeking resource rights
President Goodluck Jonathan (2010–2015) was Ijaw — first Ijaw to become Nigerian president
food: Seafood stews (fresh fish, periwinkle, crab, shrimp)
beliefs: Owu — water spirit masquerades, deeply important in Ijaw spiritual life
Egbesu — deity of war and justice, particularly among the Western Ijaw
Belief in water spirits (Oru Engemi) that inhabit rivers and seas
Sacrifices and rituals performed at waterside shrines
Juju (traditional medicine) remains influential

major.name: Kanuri
notable_facts: Descendants of the ancient Kanem-Bornu Empire — one of Africa's longest-lasting kingdoms
The Shehu of Borno is their paramount ruler
Maiduguri (Borno State capital) is their cultural heartland

major.name: Tiv
notable_facts: Known for their intricate Tiv cloth (Anger/Ange) with distinctive black and white zebra-like stripes
One of the most numerous ethnic groups in the Middle Belt
Known for the Kwagh-hir puppet theatre tradition
Richard Akura is a famous Tiv traditional leader

major.name: Ibibio
notable_facts: Known for the Ekpo masquerade society
Closely related to the Annang and Efik peoples
Akwa Ibom State is the epicenter of Ibibio culture

major.name: Edo (Bini)
major.religion: Christianity predominantly, with indigenous royal traditions
major.origin: The Edo people are the heirs of the Benin Kingdom (c. 900 CE – present), one of Africa's greatest precolonial civilizations. The Oba of Benin remains their paramount traditional ruler.
notable_facts: Home of the famous Benin Bronzes — stolen by British forces in 1897 and currently being repatriated from world museums
The Oba of Benin is one of Africa's most ancient and respected monarchies
The Igue Festival is their most important royal festival
Benin City was described by European visitors in the 16th century as one of the world's great cities

major.name: Efik
notable_facts: Calabar (Cross River State capital) is the Efik cultural heartland
Ekpe (Leopard) society — a powerful secret society with cultural and political authority
Known for the Calabar Carnival (Africa's biggest street party)
Efik cuisine (Edikang Ikong soup) is renowned across Nigeria

lingua_franca: Nigerian Pidgin (Naija) — spoken by over 75 million Nigerians as a first or second language

major_indigenous.name: Yoruba
major_indigenous.note: Also spoken in Benin Republic, Togo, and diaspora communities worldwide

major_indigenous.name: Fulfulde (Fula)
major_indigenous.region: Widespread across northern Nigeria and beyond

interesting_facts: Nigeria is the most linguistically diverse country in Africa
Nigerian Pidgin (Naija) is not officially recognized but is the most widely spoken everyday language
Each of the 36 states typically has multiple indigenous languages
The Ekpeye and Ogbia languages of the Niger Delta have fewer than 30,000 speakers each

food.summary: Nigerian cuisine is bold, aromatic, and deeply layered — built on palm oil, crayfish, fermented locust beans (dawadawa/iru), hot peppers, and an enormous variety of leafy vegetables. Every ethnic group has its own distinct culinary tradition, but shared elements run through the whole country. 'Swallows' (dough-like balls eaten by hand, never chewed) are paired with rich soups and stews across all regions.
staple_ingredients: Leafy greens (bitter leaf, ugu/pumpkin leaf, spinach, waterleaf, oha, uziza)

dishes.name: Jollof Rice
dishes.description: Nigeria's most famous dish. Rice cooked in a base of tomatoes, peppers, onions, and spices until perfectly seasoned. The subject of the West African 'Jollof Wars' with Ghana. Party Jollof (cooked over firewood) is considered the gold standard.
dishes.served_with: Fried chicken, moin moin, plantain (dodo), coleslaw

dishes.name: Pounded Yam
dishes.description: Yam boiled then pounded in a mortar until smooth and stretchy. The king of Nigerian 'swallows'. Served with any soup — egusi, bitterleaf, oha, ofe nsala. Achieving the right stretchy, smooth consistency is a point of pride.
dishes.served_with: Egusi soup, Ofe Onugbu, Ofe Nsala, Banga soup

dishes.name: Egusi Soup
dishes.origin: National (most popular in Igbo, Yoruba, and Edo cuisines)
dishes.description: Thick, hearty soup made from ground melon seeds (egusi), palm oil, assorted meats, fish, crayfish, and leafy vegetables (bitter leaf, spinach, or ugu). One of Nigeria's most beloved soups.

dishes.name: Suya
dishes.description: Nigeria's most iconic street food. Thin skewers of beef (or chicken) marinated in a spice blend (yaji) of groundnut powder, chili, ginger, paprika, and other spices, then grilled over charcoal. Served with sliced raw onion and tomato.
dishes.note: Suya spots come alive at night in every Nigerian city

dishes.name: Amala, Ewedu, and Gbegiri
dishes.description: The holy trinity of Yoruba cuisine. Amala is a dark brown swallow made from dried yam flour (elubo). Ewedu is a slimy, bright green soup made from blended jute leaves. Gbegiri is a smooth, orange-colored bean soup. The three are combined in one bowl — the famous 'Abula'.
dishes.note: This combination is particularly beloved in Ibadan and Oyo

dishes.name: Ofe Onugbu (Bitter Leaf Soup)
dishes.description: A rich, dark soup made from fresh bitter leaves (washed multiple times to reduce bitterness), cocoyam (as thickener), assorted meat, stockfish, dried fish, crayfish, and palm oil. A staple at Igbo celebrations.

dishes.name: Banga Soup
dishes.description: A rich, aromatic soup made by extracting the juice from palm nuts (banga). Has a distinctive red-orange color and is seasoned with scent leaves, oburunbebe stick, atama leaves, and other Delta-specific spices. One of Nigeria's most complex soups.
dishes.served_with: Starch (akpu), semo, or eaten with rice as 'Banga Rice'

dishes.name: Tuwo Shinkafa
dishes.description: A thick, moldable rice pudding made by mashing cooked rice until it becomes dough-like. The northern equivalent of pounded yam. Served with Northern soups.
dishes.served_with: Miyan Kuka, Miyan Taushe, Okra soup

dishes.name: Miyan Kuka
dishes.description: A distinctive Northern soup made from powdered dried baobab leaves and dried okra. Has a slimy, earthy texture and deep flavor. A staple in Hausa-Fulani households.

dishes.name: Abacha (African Salad)
dishes.description: Shredded dried cassava soaked in water, then dressed with palm oil, ugba (oil bean seeds), potash, crayfish, onion, utazi leaves, and assorted fish or meat. Served cold as a side dish or snack. A beloved Igbo street food.
dishes.note: Often served at Igbo celebrations and funerals

dishes.name: Nkwobi
dishes.description: Spicy cow foot (or cow leg) cooked in a rich sauce of palm oil, utazi leaves, potash, ehuru (calabash nutmeg), and peppers. Served in a traditional wooden bowl (orokpo). A popular bar food and restaurant delicacy.
dishes.served_with: Palm wine, beer, or pepper soup

dishes.name: Ofada Rice with Ayamase
dishes.description: Locally grown unpolished Nigerian rice with a slightly earthy, nutty flavor, served with Ayamase — a controversial green pepper-based designer stew made with green scotch bonnets, locust beans, and assorted meats. Very spicy and intensely flavorful.
dishes.note: Traditionally wrapped and served in banana leaves ('ewe ofada')

dishes.name: Edikang Ikong
dishes.description: Considered one of Nigeria's most nutritious soups. Made with a combination of ugu (pumpkin leaves) and waterleaf, cooked with assorted meats, periwinkles, dried fish, and palm oil. A celebration of the richness of Cross River cooking.

dishes.name: Moin Moin
dishes.description: Steamed bean pudding made from blended black-eyed peas, peppers, onion, and crayfish. Can be enriched with egg, fish, beef, or corned beef. Served as a side dish or main snack. Beloved across all Nigerian ethnic groups.
dishes.served_with: Jollof rice, Garri and Groundnut, or eaten alone

dishes.name: Pepper Soup
dishes.origin: National (every ethnic group has a version)
dishes.description: A light, intensely spicy broth made with aromatic pepper soup spices (ehuru, uda, uziza seeds), scotch bonnets, and various proteins — catfish, goat meat, chicken, cow leg, or offal. The ultimate Nigerian comfort food. Served at births, funerals, and cold evenings.
dishes.note: Each region has its signature version — catfish pepper soup is the most famous

dishes.name: Kilishi
dishes.description: Thin slices of beef marinated in a paste of groundnut, spices, and peppers, then dried in the sun and grilled. The northern Nigerian equivalent of beef jerky. A famous snack and souvenir from Kano.
dishes.note: Kano and Bauchi produce the finest Kilishi in Nigeria

dishes.name: Akara
dishes.origin: Yoruba (now eaten across Nigeria and West Africa)
dishes.description: Deep-fried balls of blended black-eyed peas, peppers, and onions. Crispy on the outside, soft inside. The classic Nigerian breakfast street food — eaten with pap (ogi/akamu) or bread.
dishes.note: The Yoruba also call the northern version 'Kosai'

dishes.name: Efo Riro
dishes.description: A rich vegetable stew made with tete (spinach) or soko leaves, palm oil, locust beans (iru), crayfish, assorted meat, and peppers. One of the most nutritious and beloved Yoruba dishes.

dishes.name: Okpa
dishes.description: A dense, satisfying pudding made from Bambara nut (okpa) flour, palm oil, peppers, and crayfish. Steamed in banana leaves or plastic bags. The most popular breakfast food in Enugu — sold in the streets from the early morning.
dishes.note: Known as the 'Enugu breakfast' — you cannot leave Enugu without trying Okpa

dishes.name: Garri (Eba)
dishes.description: Garri is dried, roasted cassava granules — the most consumed food in Nigeria. When soaked in cold water with groundnuts and sugar, it becomes a beloved snack. When stirred into hot water, it becomes 'Eba' — the most widely eaten swallow in Nigeria.
dishes.note: Eating Garri and Groundnut with cold water is a national experience

drinks.name: Palm Wine (Emu/Nkwu enu)
drinks.description: Naturally fermented sap tapped from oil or raffia palm trees. Slightly alcoholic, sweet when fresh, more sour as it ferments. Sacred in Igbo ceremonies.

drinks.name: Zobo (Hibiscus Drink)
drinks.description: Deep red drink made from dried hibiscus (roselle) petals, ginger, and spices. Very popular nationwide. Can be served chilled.

drinks.name: Kunu
drinks.description: A mildly fermented northern drink made from millet, sorghum, or maize. Can be plain or spiced with ginger and cloves.

drinks.name: Fura da Nono
drinks.description: Northern Nigeria's most beloved refreshment. Spiced millet balls (fura) dissolved in fresh cow's milk (nono). Sold by Fulani women across the North.

drinks.name: Pito
drinks.description: Traditional beer brewed from sorghum or millet, popular in Middle Belt and Northern Nigeria.

drinks.name: Burukutu
drinks.description: A stronger traditional beer made from fermented sorghum, particularly popular in Benue and Plateau States.

dressing.summary: Nigerian fashion is one of the most vibrant and globally influential in Africa. From hand-woven Aso-Oke to the bold Ankara prints, from the flowing Agbada to the regal Isiagu, Nigerian traditional attire carries deep cultural meaning while constantly evolving with modern global fashion trends.

national_fabrics.name: Ankara (African Wax Print)
national_fabrics.description: Brightly colored, boldly patterned wax-resist fabric. Though produced industrially (originally Dutch/Indonesian), it has become synonymous with Nigerian and African identity. Used by all ethnic groups for everything from casual to formal wear.
national_fabrics.note: The most widely used fabric in modern Nigerian fashion

national_fabrics.name: Aso-Oke
national_fabrics.description: Hand-woven prestige fabric, considered the most prestigious traditional textile in Nigeria. Comes in three classic colors: etu (deep blue-black), sanyan (beige/ivory), and alaari (rich magenta). Worn at weddings, coronations, and major ceremonies.
national_fabrics.made_in: Iseyin, Oyo State is the most famous Aso-Oke weaving center

national_fabrics.name: George Fabric
national_fabrics.origin: Used predominantly by Igbo and Niger Delta peoples
national_fabrics.description: Heavy, luxurious imported fabric (originally from India) with a distinctive sheen. Used by Igbo women for traditional weddings and formal ceremonies. Comes in many colors and patterns.

national_fabrics.name: Akwete Cloth
national_fabrics.description: Traditional hand-woven cloth from the Akwete village in Abia State. Features bold geometric patterns. Used in traditional ceremonies and increasingly in modern fashion.

national_fabrics.name: Adire
national_fabrics.description: Traditional Yoruba tie-dye fabric, typically in indigo blue. Two main techniques: Adire eleko (starch resist) and Adire oniko (tied resist). Abeokuta in Ogun State is the capital of Adire production.
national_fabrics.note: Experiencing a major global fashion revival

national_fabrics.name: Ange/Anger Cloth
national_fabrics.description: Distinctive black and white striped hand-woven cloth worn by the Tiv people. The zebra-like stripes are immediately recognizable.

men.formal: Agbada — a three-piece set of grand flowing outer robe, buba (inner top), and sokoto (trousers). Often worn with a fila (embroidered cap). The pinnacle of Yoruba male elegance.
men.everyday: Buba and sokoto with a fila
men.note: Embroidery quality and fabric type indicate social status

women.formal: Iro (wrapper) + Buba (top) + Gele (headwrap) + Ipele (shoulder shawl), made from Aso-Oke or George fabric
women.note: The Gele is an art form — skilled Gele tiers are highly sought after for weddings

men.formal: Babban Riga — a grand embroidered floor-length robe with very wide sleeves, worn over a kaftan and shokoto trousers. The embroidery (zari work) around the neck and chest indicates status.
men.note: The most elaborate embroidery can take weeks to complete by hand

women.formal: Wrapper (zani/abaya) with matching blouse and hijab/veil. Indigo-dyed fabrics are traditional.
women.henna: Intricate lalle (henna) designs on hands and feet for celebrations

men.formal: Isiagu (lion-head print shirt) + George fabric wrapper + Okpu Agu (red cap, worn by titled men only) + walking stick
men.note: The red Okpu Agu is a symbol of achievement and traditional title

women.formal: George fabric wrapper + blouse + beaded headtie + multiple strands of coral beads (Mgba) + waist beads (Jigida)
women.note: Coral bead sets are family heirlooms, passed from mother to daughter

modern_fashion.summary: Nigeria is Africa's undisputed fashion capital. Lagos hosts Africa's most influential fashion week (Lagos Fashion Week). Nigerian designers like Lisa Folawiyo, Deola Sagoe, Mai Atafo, and Adama Paris have global followings.
trends: Ankara fusion — traditional Ankara fabric in contemporary cuts
Aso-Ebi culture — matching fabric for wedding parties (now a continental trend)
Couture Gele — heavily beaded and architecturally structured headwraps
Streetwear blending Naija identity with global trends

wedding_styles.yoruba_bride: Aso-Oke fabric in buba + iro + elaborate Gele; coral beads and gold jewelry
wedding_styles.igbo_bride: Rich George fabric wrapper and blouse; beaded crown (Ogodo); coral bead necklaces and bracelets; Jigida waist beads
wedding_styles.hausa_bride: Lace or silk gown; Kamu ceremony headdress; intricate lalle (henna) on hands and feet; colorful veils
wedding_styles.national_trend: Aso-Ebi — matching fabrics worn by both families. Brides often have two different traditional wedding outfits for entrance and later change

marriage_traditions.overview: Marriage in Nigeria is fundamentally a union of two families, not just two individuals. The process typically involves multiple ceremonies — a traditional (customary) wedding, a religious ceremony (church or mosque), and often a reception. The traditional wedding is considered the most culturally significant. Bride price (a token of respect and appreciation, not a purchase) is universal across Nigerian cultures.
common_elements: Bride price / Dowry — varies by ethnic group; involves gifts of money, drinks (schnapps, wine), kola nuts, and other items presented to the bride's family
Family involvement — both extended families must consent and actively participate
Aso-Ebi — matching fabrics worn by each family's supporters
Multiple ceremony phases — introduction, traditional wedding, and religious/court wedding
Music, dance, and elaborate feasting are central to all Nigerian weddings
Kola nut — sacred at all Nigerian formal gatherings; breaking and sharing kola precedes any serious discussion

yoruba.key_ceremony: The palm wine presentation (Itori/Eru Iyawo). The bride searches for her groom among the guests carrying a cup of palm wine. When she finds him, she kneels before him and offers the cup — his drinking from it is her public acceptance of him.
yoruba.prostration: The groom and his men must prostrate fully (lie face down) before the bride's family as a sign of deep respect — a defining feature of Yoruba weddings
yoruba.bride_price: Eru Iyawo — an elaborate list including schnapps, palm wine, kola nuts, obi abata (bitter kola), orogbo (bitter berry), obi (kola nut), honey, money, fabric

igbo.key_ceremony: Igba Nkwu (wine-carrying ceremony) — the bride searches for her groom in the crowd while carrying palm wine. When she finds him, she kneels and offers him the cup. His drinking confirms acceptance. She then returns to pour wine for her father, who passes the cup to the groom.
igbo.bride_price: Formal list presentation negotiated between families; includes yams, palm wine, goats, drinks, and money. The Iku Aka (knocking) ceremony formally opens negotiations.
igbo.kola_nut: Obi (kola nut) must be presented and broken at every stage — it is the highest form of Igbo hospitality and prayer

hausa.key_ceremony: Fatihah (Nikkah) — the Islamic marriage contract. Traditionally conducted at the mosque by a Malam (Islamic scholar). The bride is represented by her Wali (guardian, usually her father).
hausa.kamu: A ceremony where the bride is unveiled to the groom's family. Women sing traditional songs and the bride's female relatives and friends celebrate.
hausa.sadaki: A token bride price paid to the bride directly, not her family — an Islamic requirement
hausa.walima: The post-wedding feast hosted by the groom's family — obligatory in Islamic tradition

beliefs.overview: Nigeria is roughly equally divided between Christianity (~50%) and Islam (~50%), with indigenous traditional beliefs remaining vital across all groups — often practiced alongside or woven into both major religions (syncretism). The saying goes: 'A Nigerian prays in the mosque in the morning, goes to church on Sunday, and consults the traditional healer when nothing else works.'

major_churches: Redeemed Christian Church of God (RCCG) — one of the world's largest churches
Mountain of Fire and Miracles Ministries
christianity.note: Nigeria is home to some of the world's largest Pentecostal churches. The Redemption Camp on the Lagos-Ibadan expressway hosts millions during annual conventions.

islam.note: 12 northern states practice Sharia law (criminal code) as of 1999–2000. Islam arrived in Hausaland from the 10th–11th century through trans-Saharan trade routes.

yoruba.name: Ifa/Orisha Tradition
yoruba.divination: Ifa — a complex divination system using a divining chain (opele) or palm nuts (ikin). Recognized by UNESCO as Intangible Cultural Heritage.
major_deities: Eshu/Elegba — deity of the crossroads, messenger of all gods
Ogun — deity of iron, war, and labor
Shango — deity of thunder and lightning, deified Oyo king
Osun — deity of the river, love, and fertility
Obatala — deity of creation and purity, maker of human bodies
Oya — deity of storms, wind, and transformation
Orunmila — deity of wisdom and knowledge, patron of Ifa divination
yoruba.masquerades: Egungun — ancestral masquerades; Gelede — masquerades honoring women's power; Oro — male-only secret society
yoruba.diaspora: Yoruba religion traveled to the Americas as Candomblé (Brazil), Santería/Lucumí (Cuba/Caribbean), and Trinidad Orisha through enslaved Yoruba people

igbo.name: Odinani
key_concepts: Ala — earth goddess, most important deity, keeper of morality
Chi — personal guardian spirit assigned at birth
Agwu — deity of divination and medicine
Amadioha — deity of thunder and lightning
Ikenga — personal deity of achievement and industry (carved wooden shrine)
igbo.masquerades: Mmanwu — masked ancestral spirits that emerge during festivals to judge the community, settle disputes, and celebrate. Feared and respected.

other.bori: A spirit possession tradition of the Hausa-Fulani that predates Islam and coexists with it
other.owu: Water spirit masquerades of the Ijaw people
other.ekpe: Leopard society of the Efik and Cross River peoples — historically the most powerful sociopolitical institution in the region
other.ogboni: A powerful secret society among the Yoruba and some Igbo, associated with earth worship and political authority

festivals.name: Osun-Osogbo Festival
festivals.location: Osogbo (Osun-Osogbo Sacred Grove, UNESCO World Heritage Site)
festivals.description: A sacred festival dedicated to Osun, the Yoruba river goddess of fertility, water, and love. Held at the 700-year-old sacred grove along the Osun River. Begins with Iwopopo (cleansing of the city), followed by the lighting of the ancient 16-point lamp, and culminates in a procession to the river shrine led by the Arugba — a virgin of royal lineage who carries a sacred calabash of offerings on her head. Attracts thousands of Osun worshippers and tourists from around the world.
highlights: Lighting of the Ina Olojumerindinlogun (500-year-old lamp)
Iboriade (crowns of past rulers assembled for blessings)
festivals.unesco_status: The Osun-Osogbo Sacred Grove is a UNESCO World Heritage Site

festivals.name: Durbar Festival
festivals.month: Eid al-Fitr (after Ramadan) and Eid al-Adha
festivals.description: One of Nigeria's most visually spectacular events. A grand royal cavalry procession in which the Emir and his horsemen, dressed in elaborate historic armor, robes, and headdresses, parade through the city. Riders demonstrate equestrian skills at full gallop before the Emir. Originally a military review, it evolved into the grandest celebration of northern Nigerian culture.
festivals.note: The Kano Durbar is the most famous and elaborate; the procession at the Kano Emirate Palace is unmissable

festivals.name: New Yam Festival (Iri Ji / Iwa Ji)
festivals.description: The most important festival in Igbo culture. Celebrates the harvest of the new yam — yam is sacred to the Igbo and no one may eat the new yam before it is formally presented to the gods and ancestors. The traditional ruler ceremonially eats the first yam of the season, followed by community feasting, traditional music, Mmanwu (masquerade) displays, and wrestling competitions. A time of communal thanksgiving and cultural pride.
highlights: Ceremonial first eating of new yam by the Eze/Obi

festivals.name: Eyo Festival (Adamu Orisha Play)
festivals.location: Lagos Island (procession from Carter Bridge to Iga Idunganran Palace)
festivals.description: One of Lagos' most iconic cultural events. Thousands of 'Eyo' masquerades dress in flowing white robes and tall hats (agogo), carrying staffs (opambata), and parade through the streets of Lagos Island. Traditionally held to honor a deceased Oba (king) or notable figure and to ease their passage to the spirit world. No hats may be worn by spectators during the festival.
highlights: Different conclaves (groups) representing different Lagos quarters
festivals.note: Widely believed to be the forerunner of the modern Brazilian carnival — through the Yoruba diaspora

festivals.name: Argungu Fishing Festival
festivals.description: One of Nigeria's oldest and most famous festivals, dating to 1934 (though its roots are much older), celebrating the end of the historic enmity between the Sokoto Caliphate and the Kebbi Kingdom. Thousands of fishermen plunge into the river simultaneously, using only traditional hand nets and calabash floats, competing to catch the largest fish within 45 minutes. The largest catch wins. Accompanied by swimming competitions, traditional music, crafts, and cultural displays.
highlights: Mass fishing competition — thousands of men enter the river simultaneously
The winner with the largest fish is celebrated
festivals.note: Fish weighing over 40kg have been caught at this festival

festivals.name: Ojude Oba Festival
festivals.description: Meaning 'Festival in the King's Court', this annual festival was established in 1892 when the Ijebu Muslims celebrated the Awujale's benevolence in granting them land for a mosque. Age-grade groups (Regberegbes) parade in matching outfits before the Awujale (king of Ijebu), paying homage with music, dance, and horseback riding. Known for spectacular displays of Yoruba fashion — described as a 'fashion runway meets royal court'.
highlights: Regberegbe age-grade parades in matching traditional attire
Prayers and homage to the Awujale

festivals.name: Calabar Carnival
festivals.description: Billed as 'Africa's Biggest Street Party'. A month-long carnival and cultural festival showcasing Nigerian creativity through music, dance, elaborate costumes, and cultural performances. Competing carnival bands parade through Calabar's streets in extravagant costumes. Draws hundreds of thousands of visitors annually.
highlights: Competing carnival bands in spectacular costumes
festivals.note: The Calabar Carnival is the most commercially significant festival in Nigeria in terms of tourism

festivals.name: Igue Festival
festivals.description: The most sacred royal festival of the Benin Kingdom. The Oba of Benin performs ancient rituals to renew his spiritual powers and bless his subjects for the coming year. Involves elaborate royal processions, ancestral prayers, and the display of the Oba's royal regalia. Chieftaincy titles may be conferred. A living link to the ancient Benin Kingdom's traditions.

festivals.name: Sango Festival (World Sango Festival)
festivals.location: Oyo (Palace of the Alaafin of Oyo)
festivals.description: Annual festival honoring Shango, the Yoruba deity of thunder and lightning, and the 4th Alaafin (King) of Oyo who was deified. The festival is observed in over 40 countries worldwide where the Yoruba diaspora spread (particularly Brazil, Cuba, and the Caribbean). Features possession rituals by Sango devotees, fire displays, thunderstone artifacts, and elaborate traditional music.

festivals.name: Ofala Festival
festivals.description: One of the oldest Igbo royal festivals. Celebrated by the Obi (king) of Onitsha to mark the beginning of a new Igbo year (Igu Aro), affirm his authority, and bless his people. The Obi appears in full regalia to receive homage from his subjects. Chieftaincy titles are conferred. Features ancestral masquerades, traditional dances, and a display of wealth and cultural pride.
highlights: Obi of Onitsha in full royal regalia

festivals.name: Olojo Festival
festivals.description: One of the most ancient Yoruba festivals, celebrating 'the day of the first dawn' — the day Ogun, the first son of Oduduwa, descended to earth with iron tools. The Ooni of Ife (spiritual head of all Yoruba) emerges from seclusion wearing the sacred Are Crown — worn only once a year. The Are Crown is considered the most sacred regalia in all of Yorubaland.
highlights: Ooni of Ife wearing the sacred Are Crown (once per year)

music_and_pop_culture.summary: Nigeria is the undisputed music capital of Africa. Afrobeats — the global genre born from Lagos — has conquered the world's major music charts, collaborated with international superstars, and brought African music to billions of listeners. Nigeria also birthed Nollywood, the world's second-largest film industry by volume.

genres.name: Afrobeats
genres.description: The dominant genre of the 21st century — an umbrella term for contemporary Nigerian pop music blending highlife, R&B, hip-hop, dancehall, and electronic music with distinctly Nigerian rhythms and melodies. Born in Lagos, it now dominates global music charts.
pioneers: Fela Kuti (Afrobeat — the original, with a 't')
genres.global_impact: Collaborated with Beyoncé, Drake, Ed Sheeran, Justin Bieber, Chris Brown, and many others. Dominated Spotify and Apple Music charts globally.

genres.name: Afrobeat (Original)
genres.description: The original genre created by Fela Kuti — a fusion of Yoruba music, jazz, funk, highlife, and politically charged lyrics. A weapon against military dictatorship and social injustice. Different from modern 'Afrobeats'. Fela is Nigeria's greatest musical legend.

genres.name: Highlife
genres.description: One of the oldest West African popular genres, blending traditional Nigerian rhythms with Western brass band music. Developed in the early 20th century, popular in Igbo and Yoruba communities. The grandfather of modern Afrobeats.

genres.name: Jùjú Music
genres.description: A Yoruba guitar-based popular music genre developed in the 1920s. Characterised by talking drums, guitar, and Yoruba vocals. Made famous internationally by King Sunny Ade.

genres.name: Fuji Music
genres.description: Yoruba Muslim music genre born from Ajisari (Muslim pre-dawn wake-up calls during Ramadan). Very fast-paced, percussive, and competitive. Hugely popular in Lagos.

genres.name: Naija Hip-Hop
genres.description: Nigerian hip-hop that blends American rap with Pidgin English, Yoruba, Igbo, and Hausa. Emerged in the 1990s–2000s and continues to grow.

genres.name: Afropop / Afroswing
genres.description: Lighter, danceable pop music with African melodies and contemporary production. Hugely popular in clubs and streaming platforms.

genres.name: Apala
genres.description: Traditional Yoruba Muslim percussion music, using the gangan (talking drum) and sekere (beaded gourd rattle). Developed by Haruna Ishola in the 1940s.

legendary_artists.name: Fela Anikulapo Kuti
legendary_artists.legacy: The greatest Nigerian musician of all time. A revolutionary artist and activist who used music to fight military dictatorship. Founded the Kalakuta Republic commune. His music influenced hip-hop, funk, jazz, and contemporary Afrobeats globally. His son Femi Kuti and grandson Made Kuti carry on the tradition.

legendary_artists.name: King Sunny Ade
legendary_artists.legacy: Brought Jùjú music to global audiences. Grammy-nominated. Known as the 'Minister of Enjoyment'.

legendary_artists.name: Miriam Makeba (honorary mention — sang Nigerian music)
legendary_artists.note: While South African, Makeba championed African music globally

current_stars.name: Burna Boy
current_stars.achievement: Grammy Award winner (2021, Best Global Music Album — 'Twice as Tall'). Self-styled 'African Giant'.

current_stars.name: Wizkid
current_stars.achievement: Grammy Award winner (2021, Best Music Video — 'Brown Skin Girl' with Beyoncé). 'Starboy'.

current_stars.name: Davido
current_stars.achievement: One of Afrobeats' biggest global stars. Known for anthems like 'Fall', 'IF', 'Assurance'.

current_stars.name: Tems
current_stars.achievement: Grammy Award winner (2023, Best Melodic Rap Performance — with Future & Drake). First Nigerian female Grammy winner.

current_stars.name: Asake
current_stars.achievement: One of the fastest-rising artists of the 2020s. Known for blending Fuji rhythms with contemporary pop.

current_stars.name: Rema
current_stars.achievement: 'Calm Down' became one of the most-streamed Afrobeats songs globally, duetting with Selena Gomez.

current_stars.name: Ckay
current_stars.achievement: 'Love Nwantiti' became the most-streamed African song in history (2021).

current_stars.name: Ayra Starr
current_stars.achievement: One of the leading voices of the new generation of Nigerian female artists.

current_stars.name: Olamide
current_stars.achievement: The king of the Nigerian streets. Built YBNL Nation label. Discovered Fireboy DML, Asake, and many others.

current_stars.name: Falz
current_stars.achievement: Nigeria's most socially conscious rapper. Son of human rights lawyer Femi Falana. Known for 'This is Nigeria'.

nollywood.name: Nollywood
nollywood.rank: World's 2nd largest film industry by volume (number of films produced annually)
nollywood.note: Nollywood produces approximately 2,500 films per year — surpassed only by Bollywood (India)
nollywood.history: Nollywood began in 1992 with the release of 'Living in Bondage' (directed by Chris Obi Rapu), which pioneered the direct-to-video format that made films affordable and widely accessible
nollywood.global_impact: Netflix has invested heavily in Nollywood. 'Lion Heart' by Genevieve Nnaji (2018) was the first Nigerian film selected for the Oscars foreign film category. 'Lionheart' was acquired by Netflix in 2018 for a record $3.8 million.

comedy.note: Nigerian stand-up comedy and skit-making has exploded globally through social media

geopolitical_zones.notable: Kano is the commercial hub of northern Nigeria; Sokoto is the seat of the Sokoto Caliphate

geopolitical_zones.dominant_group: Kanuri (Borno), Fulani, Hausa, and over 40 smaller groups
geopolitical_zones.notable: Borno State was the seat of the Kanem-Bornu Empire; one of Africa's longest-lasting kingdoms

geopolitical_zones.dominant_groups: Tiv (Benue), Igala (Kogi), Nupe (Niger), Gbagyi, many others
geopolitical_zones.notable: Lokoja (Kogi) is where the Niger and Benue Rivers meet — historic first capital of Nigeria; Jos (Plateau) is known as Nigeria's 'home of peace and tourism'
Lagos is Africa's most populous city and financial capital; Ile-Ife (Osun State) is the spiritual homeland of the Yoruba; Ibadan is one of Africa's largest cities by area
Anambra is Nigeria's leading commercial state after Lagos; Nnewi is Nigeria's industrial hub; Owerri is a major entertainment city ('Owu Capital of Nigeria')

geopolitical_zones.dominant_groups: Ijaw, Ibibio, Efik, Edo (Bini), Urhobo, Isoko, Itsekiri, Ogoni, Annang
geopolitical_zones.notable: This zone produces the bulk of Nigeria's oil wealth. Port Harcourt is the oil capital. Benin City is home to the ancient Benin Kingdom. Calabar is Nigeria's tourism capital.

proverbs_and_wisdom.proverb: Ọjọ́ tí a bá ń sunkún, a máa ń rọra.
proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: The day we cry is usually a slow day.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Grief slows everything down; take life one step at a time.

proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: He who brings kola nut brings life.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Hospitality and respect are the foundation of life and community.

proverbs_and_wisdom.proverb: Mutum ya fi duniya girma, amma duniya ba ta fi shi ba.
proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: A man may consider himself greater than the world, but the world is not smaller than him.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Humility is essential — no man is bigger than the world around him.

proverbs_and_wisdom.proverb: Agwa bụ mma — Character is beauty.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: True beauty lies in a person's character, not their appearance.

proverbs_and_wisdom.proverb: Bi a bá mọ ibí tí ìpàdé wà, a óò máa yára pàdé.
proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: If we know where rain would beat us, we would not allow it.

proverbs_and_wisdom.proverb: Ina wuta a kan ransa — Fire burns on its own fuel.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Anger and conflict consume those who start them.
There is power in numbers and community solidarity.

proverbs_and_wisdom.proverb: Ẹni tó bá pa ẹja ìgbà oru, kì í jẹ ẹ ní òwurọ̀.
proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: He who kills a fish at night does not eat it in the morning.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Patience — the fruits of hard work may not come immediately.

travel_and_places.note: Nigeria has some of West Africa's most compelling destinations — from ancient kingdoms to modern megacities, rainforests, savannas, and a stunning coastline.

must_visit.name: Osun-Osogbo Sacred Grove
must_visit.description: A sacred forest along the Osun River, one of the last such groves that once bordered all Yoruba cities. Home to shrines, sculptures, and the annual Osun-Osogbo Festival. A UNESCO World Heritage Site and a deeply spiritual place.

must_visit.name: Lagos
must_visit.description: Africa's most populous city and its undisputed pop culture capital. Home to the Afrobeats industry, Nollywood, incredible street food, Victoria Island's nightlife, the Lekki Conservation Centre, Nike Art Gallery, and Eko Atlantic (the new city being built into the ocean).

must_visit.name: Benin City & Benin Kingdom
must_visit.description: One of Africa's greatest precolonial cities. The seat of the ancient Benin Kingdom, famed for the Benin Bronzes (some of Africa's greatest artworks, currently being repatriated from world museums). The Oba's Palace and the National Museum Benin are must-visits.

must_visit.name: Yankari National Park
must_visit.description: Nigeria's premier game reserve and one of the finest in West Africa. Home to elephants, lions, baboons, warthogs, buffalo, and over 350 bird species. Also features the Wikki Warm Springs — natural thermal pools perfect for swimming.
must_visit.highlight: Wikki Warm Springs — crystal-clear natural thermal springs

must_visit.name: Abuja
must_visit.description: Nigeria's purpose-built capital (opened 1991). Home to Aso Rock (the distinctive granite monolith), the Nigerian National Mosque and National Christian Centre, the Zuma Rock (nicknamed 'the gateway to Abuja'), Millennium Park, and the Transcorp Hilton.

must_visit.name: Obudu Mountain Resort
must_visit.description: One of Nigeria's most beautiful natural destinations, located on the Obudu Plateau near the Cameroon border at 1,576m elevation. Features lush highland vegetation, a cable car ride, waterfalls, and cool temperatures — a refreshing escape from the heat.
must_visit.note: Often called 'Nigeria's Switzerland' for its cool highland climate

must_visit.name: Ile-Ife
must_visit.description: The spiritual homeland of the Yoruba people — where Oduduwa is said to have created the earth. Home to the Ooni's Palace, the Ife Museum (containing priceless terracotta and bronze heads), the Ore Grove (ancient sacred site), and Obafemi Awolowo University (one of Africa's most beautiful campuses).
must_visit.note: Every Yoruba festival has roots in Ile-Ife

must_visit.name: Kano
must_visit.description: Nigeria's second-largest city and one of Africa's oldest and most historic cities. Home to the ancient Kano City Walls (15th century), the Gidan Makama Museum, the Kurmi Market (one of West Africa's oldest markets, trading for over 1,000 years), and the famous Kofar Mata Dye Pits (dyeing indigo cloth for centuries).

must_visit.name: Cross River National Park (Oban Hills)
must_visit.description: Part of the most biodiverse rainforest in West Africa. Home to chimpanzees, forest elephants, drill monkeys, and hundreds of bird species. One of Nigeria's most important conservation areas.
must_visit.note: The Drill Ranch at Calabar rehabilitates endangered drill monkeys

budget_tips: Lagos to Abuja by bus (₦5,000–₦8,000) is cheaper than flying (₦25,000+) but takes 8–10 hours
Buka (local canteen) meals are the most authentic and affordable — from ₦500–₦2,000 per plate
Dangote Cement-colored danfo buses in Lagos are the cheapest way to move around the city
The best suya is always from roadside spots after dark — not restaurants
safety_tips: Abuja and Port Harcourt GRA are generally the safest areas for visitors
Avoid driving at night on unfamiliar interstate routes
Use only licensed taxis or ride-hailing apps (Bolt, InDriver) in major cities
Keep vigilant in markets and crowded areas — pickpocketing exists in busy spots
Always respect local customs — dress modestly when visiting mosques or traditional palaces
